title: The Boy Who Couldn't Walk
author: Fablewick
lesson: Courage isn't standing up. Courage is showing up anyway.
summary: Franklin loved the ocean more than anything. Then one summer, an illness took his legs. The story of how he learned to lead a country from a chair.

1.label: Little Listener
1.text:
Franklin loved the ocean.

He loved the wind. He loved the waves. He loved the cold, cold water.

Every summer he sailed his little boat. He swam until his fingers turned white. He climbed the wet rocks with his strong, quick legs.

"You are made of wind and water," his mother said.

Franklin grew up. He worked hard. He wanted to help people.

Then one summer, Franklin got sick. Very, very sick.

His legs stopped listening.

"You will not walk again," the doctor said.

Franklin was quiet. Quiet for a long, long time.

Then he said, "That is one thing. There are many other things."

So Franklin worked. He made his arms strong. He made his shoulders strong. He learned to ride his wheelchair like a little boat. Something to steer.

Far away, the people of his country were scared. The years were hard and long.

Franklin became their president. At night he talked to them by the fire, slow and warm. "You are not alone," he said. "You will be all right."

Franklin never walked again.

But every day, he showed up.

And showing up, it turned out, was the bravest thing of all.

2.label: New Reader
2.text:
Franklin loved the ocean.

Every summer, when Franklin was a boy, his family went to a green island called Campobello, where the sea was so cold it made your bones hum. Franklin learned to sail before he learned to write neatly. He swam until his fingers turned white. He climbed the wet dark rocks barefoot, and his legs were strong and quick and sure.

"You are made of wind and water," his mother told him.

Franklin believed her. It felt true.

He grew up. He married a thoughtful woman named Eleanor. He went into politics, because he wanted to help people. Really help, not just say so.

Then one summer he came back to Campobello, a grown man now. He dove off the rocks into the same cold water he had loved his whole life. He laughed with his children on the shore. He went to bed tired, a good kind of tired.

In the morning, his legs would not wake up.

The sickness was called polio. Doctors came and went. They tried everything they knew. At last one of them said the words very gently: "You will not walk again."

Franklin was quiet for a long time. Eleanor sat beside him and held his hand. Outside the window, the sea went on sounding the way the sea always sounds — far away, calm, patient.

Then Franklin spoke.

"That is one thing," he said. "There are many other things."

So he worked. Not at running. Not at jumping. Those doors were closed. He worked at the parts of him that still listened — his shoulders, his arms, his chest. He lifted his whole body between two wooden bars, morning after morning after morning. He swam in pools of warm water, where his legs could remember what moving felt like. He learned to use his wheelchair like a tool. Like a boat, almost. Something to steer.

Years went by. His country fell into trouble: money trouble first, then a great war far across the sea. People were scared. People were hungry. People did not know if things would ever be all right.

Franklin ran for president. He won.

And at night he sat by the fire in his big office and spoke into the radio, slow and warm, like a friend at the kitchen table. In little lit houses everywhere, people leaned in to listen.

"You are not alone," he told them. "We will get through this together."

Franklin never walked again.

But every single day, he showed up.

And showing up, it turned out, was the bravest thing of all.

3.label: On Your Own
3.text:
Franklin Delano Roosevelt loved the ocean more than almost anything in the world.

Every summer, when Franklin was a boy, his family sailed to a green island called Campobello, off the coast of Canada, where the water was so cold it made your bones hum and the sky was so wide it made you feel wonderfully small. Franklin learned to sail before he learned to write a proper letter. He swam until his fingers turned white and his teeth chattered like little drums. He climbed the wet, dark rocks barefoot. He could row a heavy wooden boat all by himself while other boys his age could barely lift the oars.

One afternoon his mother watched him pull a sail tight against the wind.

"You are made of wind and water, Franklin," she said.

Franklin believed her, because it felt true. His legs were strong. His arms were stronger. His chest was full of cold salt air.

He grew up, the way boys do. He went to school, and then to more school. He married a thoughtful, serious woman named Eleanor, who would turn out to be one of the great people of his life, though neither of them knew it yet. He went into politics, because he believed politics was a place where you could truly help people, not just say you wanted to.

And then one summer, when Franklin was thirty-nine and had five children of his own, he came back to Campobello. He dove off the dark rocks into the same cold water he had loved his whole life. He raced his children along the shore, laughing. He went to bed tired, a good, ordinary kind of tired.

When he woke the next morning, his legs would not wake up with him.

The sickness was called polio.

It was a long, frightening summer. Eleanor stayed close. Doctors came and went, trying hot baths and cold baths, exercises and braces, everything they knew. And after many weeks, one of them finally said the words Franklin had already begun to suspect, in the gentle voice doctors keep for the hardest things:

"Mr. Roosevelt, you will not walk again."

Franklin was quiet for a very long time. Eleanor was quiet beside him, holding his hand. Outside the tall window, the sea went on sounding the way the sea had always sounded: far away, and calm, and patient.

At last Franklin spoke.

"That," he said slowly, "is one thing. There are many other things."

What happened next did not happen quickly. It took years.

He worked. Not at running, not at jumping. Those doors were closed now. He worked at the parts of his body that still answered when he called. His shoulders. His arms. His chest. Every morning he lifted his whole weight between two wooden bars, arms shaking, jaw set, again and again and again. He swam in pools of warm water, because warm water was one of the few places his legs could remember what moving felt like. He even bought a place called Warm Springs, where the water stayed warm all year round, and turned it into a place where other children with polio could come and swim and grow strong too.

He learned to use his wheelchair like a tool. Like a boat, almost. Something to steer, not something to be stuck in. He learned to lift himself in and out of a car with nothing but his arms. He learned to stand for a few minutes at a time on heavy metal braces, holding tight to a podium, smiling out at a crowd that never guessed what it cost him.

And he learned something else, something that took the longest of all: how to lead a room from a chair exactly the way he used to lead one standing up. He did it by being the calmest person in it.

Then his country needed him.

A great money-storm swept the land. The Depression, the newspapers called it. Banks closed their doors. Farms went quiet. People who had worked hard their whole lives could not buy bread. And just as that storm began to lift, a war rose up across the ocean, bigger than any war before it. People were frightened. People were tired. People were no longer sure that things could be all right.

Franklin ran for president. He won — and won again, and again, and again. Four times. More than anyone in his country's history, before or since.

At night he sat in his chair by the fire in his big office and spoke into the radio, slow and warm, like a friend at the kitchen table. He called these talks his Fireside Chats. In small lit houses all across the country, families leaned close to listen.

"You are not alone," he told them. He told them the only thing they truly had to fear was fear itself. He told them, in a hundred different ways, that they were going to be all right.

Franklin Delano Roosevelt never walked again.

But every single day — through the long storm, and the long war, and a tiredness deep enough to drown a quieter man — he showed up.

And showing up, it turned out, was the bravest thing of all.
